title: Rush drop-in chair seat
subtitle: A traditional rush seat on a removable panel frame
excerpt: A rush seat woven onto a removable drop-in frame panel that fits inside a chair seat opening. English rush twisted into continuous strands, worked over-and-under across the frame rails. The same method as a fixed rush seat, made portable by working on a separate panel.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for rush-seating technique. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the rush-work domestic tradition. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on rush-seating in British domestic chair-making.

glossaryTerms.definition: In rush work, the over-and-under weave used to fill the seat area after the corner triangles are established.
A pointed steel rod for opening a path through the weave to push in new material or to pack rows tight.
In rush work, a strengthening weave worked at the outer edge of the frame to hold the perimeter rows firm.

content.text: A drop-in chair seat is a rush seat built on a separate wooden frame panel that slides into the seat opening of a chair. The panel can be replaced or repaired without dismantling the chair. It is also a good first rush project because the frame sits on the workbench rather than being worked in position on a chair.
The panel frame

Build a panel frame that fits the chair seat opening with 5 mm clearance all round. A section of 20 mm x 20 mm is sufficient for a seat panel. Mortise-and-tenon corners give the most rigid frame. The frame must sit level inside the chair opening, supported by a rebate or shelf inside the chair rails.
Preparing the rush

Soak dried English rush in the trough for one to two hours. Weight bundles down if they float; the rush must be fully submerged. The rush is ready when it bends without snapping. Work one bundle at a time and keep unused rush wrapped in a damp cloth to slow drying during the session.
Working the corner triangles

Tie the first rush strand to one corner of the frame. Bring it across to the adjacent rail, wrap under the rail and back, then work to the next corner. Each four-wrap sequence fills one circuit of all four corners. Use the
bodkin
to push each new row under the previous rows at the corners, keeping the rows tight and flat. Continue until the corner triangles meet at the midpoint of each rail.
Filling the centre
Once the corner triangles have closed all four rails, a rectangular gap remains in the centre. Fill it with
randing

: pass the rush over the front rail, under and across to the back rail, over the back rail, and back to the front. Pack each completed row toward the corner area with the bodkin before adding the next.
Joining rush strands

Join a new strand by knotting the butt end of the new strand to the tip end of the old one on the underside of the seat. Trim the knot tails to 10 mm with secateurs. The knots are hidden once the seat dries and firms up.
Finishing and fitting
Tie off the final strand on the underside. Work a
waling

row around the frame perimeter if the outer edge of the weave looks loose. Let the seat dry fully for two to three weeks in a ventilated room. The rush tightens as it dries. When dry, slip the panel into the chair seat opening; a well-fitted panel holds in place by friction.
Variations
Sea-grass substitute: pre-twisted sea-grass on a roll replaces loose rush. No soaking needed; join new lengths by splicing.
Stool seat: the same technique on a square stool frame, without the chair-fitting requirement. A good first rush project.

rows.symptom: Rush tears during tight corner wrapping
rows.cause: Rush strand has dried out during the session
rows.fix: Re-soak the strand for ten minutes. Keep unused rush wrapped in a damp cloth throughout the session.

rows.symptom: Panel rocks in the chair seat opening
rows.cause: Frame is too small for the opening
rows.fix: Glue thin strips of oak to the outer edge of the frame to make up the gap. Sand to a close fit, aiming for finger-tight in the opening.
